text:
'I see-her ladyship's waiting-maid. Well, the temptation of sudden wealth so easily acquired was too much for you, as it has been for better men before you; but you were not very scrupulous in the means you used. It seems to me, Ryder, that there is the making of a very pretty villain in you. You knew that this man Horner, the plumber, had been concerned in some such matter before, and that suspicion would rest the more readily upon him. What did you do, then? You made some small job in my lady's room-you and your confederate Cusack-and you managed that he should be the man sent for. Then, when he had left, you rifled the jewel-case, raised the alarm, and had this unfortunate man arrested. You then-'  
Ryder threw himself down suddenly upon the rug and clutched at my companion's knees. 'For God's sake, have mercy!' he shrieked. 'Think of my father! Of my mother! It would break their hearts. I never went wrong before! I never will again. I swear it. I'll swear it on a Bible. Oh, don't bring it into court! For Christ's sake, don't!'  
'Get back into your chair!' said Holmes sternly. 'It is very well to cringe and crawl now, but you thought little enough of this poor Horner in the dock for a crime of which he knew nothing.'  
'I will fly, Mr. Holmes. I will leave the country, sir. Then the charge against him will break down.'  
'Hum! We will talk about that. And now let us hear a true account of the next act. How came the stone into the goose, and how came the goose into the open market? Tell us the truth, for there lies your only hope of safety.'  
Ryder passed his tongue over his parched lips. 'I will tell you it just as it happened, sir,' said he. 'When Horner had been arrested, it seemed to me that it would be best for me to get away with the stone at once, for I did not know at what moment the police might not take it into their heads to search me and my room. There was no place about the hotel where it would be safe. I went out, as if on some commission, and I made for my sister's house. She had married a man named Oakshott, and lived in Brixton Road, where she fattened fowls for the market. All the way there every man I met seemed to me to be a policeman or a detective; and, for all that it was a cold night, the sweat was pouring down my face before I came to the Brixton Road. My sister asked me what was the matter, and why I was so pale; but I told her that I had been upset by the jewel robbery at the hotel. Then I went into the back yard and smoked a pipe and wondered what it would be best to do.  
'I had a friend once called Maudsley, who went to the bad, and has just been serving his time in Pentonville. One day he had met me, and fell into talk about the ways of thieves, and how they could get rid of what they stole. I knew that he would be true to me, for I knew one or two things about him; so I made up my mind to go right on to Kilburn, where he lived, and take him into my confidence. He would show me how to turn the stone into money. But how to get to him in safety? I thought of the agonies I had gone through in coming from the hotel. I might at any moment be seized and searched, and there would be the stone in my waistcoat pocket. I was leaning against the wall at the time and looking at the geese which were waddling about round my feet, and suddenly an idea came into my head which showed me how I could beat the best detective that ever lived.  
'My sister had told me some weeks before that I might have the pick of her geese for a Christmas present, and I knew that she was always as good as her word. I would take my goose now, and in it I would carry my stone to Kilburn. There was a little shed in the yard, and behind this I drove one of the birds-a fine big one, white, with a barred tail. I caught it, and prying its bill open, I thrust the stone down its throat as far as my finger could reach. The bird gave a gulp, and I felt the stone pass along its gullet and down into its crop. But the creature flapped and struggled, and out came my sister to know what was the matter. As I turned to speak to her the brute broke loose and fluttered off among the others.  
- ' 'Whatever were you doing with that bird, Jem?' says she.
- ''Well,' said I, 'you said you'd give me one for Christmas, and I was feeling which was the fattest.'
- ''Oh,' says she, 'we've set yours aside for you-Jem's bird, we call it. It's the big white one over yonder. There's twenty-six of them, which makes one for you, and one for us, and two dozen for the market.'
- ''Thank you, Maggie,' says I; 'but if it is all the same to you, I'd rather have that one I was handling just now.'
- ''The other is a good three pound heavier,' said she, 'and we fattened it expressly for you.'
- ''Never mind. I'll have the other, and I'll take it now,' said I.
- ''Oh, just as you like,' said she, a little huffed. 'Which is it you want, then?'
- ' 'That white one with the barred tail, right in the middle of the flock.'
- ' 'Oh, very well. Kill it and take it with you.'  
'Well, I did what she said, Mr. Holmes, and I carried the bird all the way to Kilburn. I told my pal what I had done, for he was a man that it was easy to tell a thing like that to. He laughed until he choked, and we got a knife and opened the goose. My heart turned to water, for there was no sign of the stone, and I knew that some terrible mistake had occurred. I left the bird, rushed back to my sister's, and hurried into the back yard. There was not a bird to be seen there.  
- ' 'Where are they all, Maggie?' I cried.
- ' 'Gone to the dealer's, Jem.'
- ' 'Which dealer's?'
- ' 'Breckinridge, of Covent Garden.'
- ''But was there another with a barred tail?' I asked, 'the same as the one I chose?'
- ' 'Yes, Jem; there were two barred-tailed ones, and I could never tell them apart.'  
'Well, then, of course I saw it all, and I ran off as hard as my feet would carry me to this man Breckinridge; but he had sold the lot at once, and not one word would he tell me as to where they had gone. You heard him yourselves to-night. Well, he has always answered me like that. My sister thinks that I am going mad. Sometimes I think that I am myself. And now-and now I am myself a branded thief, without ever having touched the wealth for which I sold my character. God help me! God help me!' He burst into convulsive sobbing, with his face buried in his hands.  
There was a long silence, broken only by his heavy breathing and by the measured tapping of Sherlock Holmes' finger-tips upon the edge of the table. Then my friend rose and threw open the door.  
'Get out!' said he.  
'What, sir! Oh, Heaven bless you!'  
'No more words. Get out!'  
And no more words were needed. There was a rush, a clatter upon the stairs, the bang of a door, and the crisp rattle of running footfalls from the street.  
'After all, Watson,' said Holmes, reaching up his hand for his clay pipe, 'I am not retained by the police to supply their deficiencies. If Horner were in danger it would be another thing; but this fellow will not appear against him, and the case must collapse. I suppose that I am commuting a felony, but it is just possible that I am saving a soul. This fellow will not go wrong again; he is too terribly frightened. Send him to jail now, and you make him a jail-bird for life.
name: Chunk 6/7

motivations: prevent a man from becoming a career criminal
properties.narrative_arc: Uncovers the full truth, then chooses moral intervention over strict legal punishment, releasing the culprit to save his soul

motivations: steal the jewel for sudden wealth
avoid prison and save his family's honor
properties.narrative_arc: Confesses to Holmes, hides the jewel in a goose, loses it due to a mix-up, breaks down, and is released; left terrified but presumably reformed
properties.distinctive_qualities: A 'branded thief' who never actually touched the wealth; his extreme fear becomes his salvation

personality_traits: previously involved in minor similar matters
properties.narrative_arc: Arrested for the jewel robbery due to a frame-up; the case against him collapses when Ryder is caught and released
properties.distinctive_qualities: Target of suspicion due to past record; victim of a deliberate frame-up

properties.narrative_arc: Helps Ryder frame Horner and steal the jewel; mentioned as part of the initial crime setup
properties.distinctive_qualities: Ryder's partner in the theft and framing

motivations: provide for her household and sell fowls to the market
properties.narrative_arc: Gives Ryder a goose, unaware of his crime; her practical mistake (mixing up geese) causes Ryder's downfall
properties.distinctive_qualities: Fattens fowls for the market; refers to the chosen goose as 'Jem's bird'

properties.narrative_arc: Mentioned only as Maggie's husband; his house in Brixton Road serves as Ryder's temporary hideout
properties.distinctive_qualities: Absent from the scene; represents the domestic setting where the crime's aftermath unfolds

personality_traits: reliable to Ryder due to leverage
motivations: help Ryder turn the stolen stone into money
properties.narrative_arc: Served time in Pentonville; Ryder seeks him out in Kilburn to fence the jewel; they open the goose but find the stone missing
properties.distinctive_qualities: Lives in Kilburn; knows the ways of thieves; holds secrets over him that ensure his cooperation

properties.narrative_arc: Buys the geese from Maggie's yard, sells them immediately, refuses to reveal where they went, blocking Ryder's recovery of the stone
properties.distinctive_qualities: Operates in Covent Garden; known for giving one-word answers or silence to inquiries

properties.narrative_arc: Witnesses the confession and Holmes's decision to release Ryder; serves as the audience surrogate
properties.distinctive_qualities: Referred to as 'my friend' by Holmes; present during the interrogation

properties.physical_description: Hotel room belonging to a lady
properties.narrative_function: Crime scene where the jewel was stolen; setting for the initial theft and frame-up
properties.symbolic_meaning: Represents vulnerability and the target of greed

properties.physical_description: Residential street where Maggie Oakshott lives
properties.narrative_function: Hideout for Ryder; location of the back yard where the goose is handled
Destination for Ryder to fence the jewel; site of the failed goose opening
Location of Breckinridge's poultry business; where the stolen geese are sold
properties.symbolic_meaning: Hub of commerce and disposal of stolen goods

properties.narrative_function: Reference point for Maudsley's criminal record; represents the fate Ryder fears
properties.symbolic_meaning: Institutional punishment and lifelong criminality ('jail-bird for life')

properties.who_owns_uses_it: Originally the lady's; stolen by Ryder; hidden in a goose; lost
properties.narrative_role: MacGuffin / central mystery object; catalyst for the crime
properties.symbolic_meaning: Temptation of sudden wealth; moral test
properties.significance: Mysterious/lost (hidden in goose, then lost when goose was swapped/sold)

properties.physical_description: A large white bird with a barred tail; one of twenty-six fattened fowls
properties.who_owns_uses_it: Maggie Oakshott (fattened for market); Ryder (used as container); Maudsley (opened)
properties.narrative_role: Plot device / container for the stolen jewel; cause of the mix-up
properties.significance: Resolved (stone was inside, but lost due to swap; goose opened but found empty)

properties.narrative_role: Symbol of Ryder's desperation and plea for mercy
properties.symbolic_meaning: Plea for divine intervention and mercy; contrasts with his crime

properties.narrative_role: Character prop; used during Holmes's contemplation
properties.symbolic_meaning: Holmes's contemplative state; transition to resolution

properties.what_changed: Jewel stolen; Horner framed and arrested
properties.immediate_consequence: Alarm raised; Horner arrested; Ryder flees with the stone
properties.why_it_matters: Sets the entire plot in motion

properties.what_changed: Innocent plumber imprisoned; case built against him
properties.cause: Ryder's frame-up using Horner's past record
properties.immediate_consequence: Police focus on Horner; Ryder flees to Kilburn
properties.why_it_matters: Creates the moral/legal dilemma Holmes must resolve

properties.what_changed: Ryder's full crime revealed; his psychological breakdown
properties.immediate_consequence: Holmes decides to release Ryder; case against Horner collapses
properties.why_it_matters: Climax of the investigation; moral resolution

properties.what_changed: Ryder freed; Holmes commutes a felony to save a soul
properties.cause: Holmes's belief that Ryder is too frightened to reoffend
properties.immediate_consequence: Ryder leaves; Holmes reflects on saving a soul vs. supplying police deficiencies
properties.why_it_matters: Thematic resolution; establishes Holmes's moral code

properties.structure: Investigative force; relies on evidence and suspects
properties.purpose/goal: Solve the jewel robbery, arrest the culprit
properties.status: Active, but deficient in this case
properties.plot_impact: Holmes supplies their deficiencies; their case collapses when Horner is released

properties.structure: Commercial network of dealers and farmers
properties.plot_impact: The mechanism through which the stolen goose (and stone) is lost; represents the commercial disposal of stolen goods

properties.manifestation: Ryder's temptation to steal the jewel; the driving motive for the crime
properties.resolution: Ryder never actually touches the wealth; it is lost due to a mix-up

properties.manifestation: Holmes's decision to release Ryder; Ryder's plea for mercy; the idea of saving a soul
properties.resolution: Holmes chooses moral salvation over strict legal punishment, believing fear will reform Ryder

properties.manifestation: Horner's past record making him an easy target; Ryder's fear of being 'branded a thief'
properties.resolution: Ryder's reputation is destroyed, but he is spared legal branding; Horner's reputation is cleared by the collapse of the case

properties.manifestation: The theft of the jewel; the legal charge against Ryder; the concept of a 'jail-bird for life'
properties.resolution: Legally unresolved (case collapses), but morally addressed through Holmes's intervention

relationships.description: Holmes interrogates and assesses Ryder's criminal nature and motives.
relationships.context: 'I see-her ladyship's waiting-maid. Well, the temptation of sudden wealth so easily acquired was too much for you, as it has been for better men before you; but you were not very scrupulous in the means you used. It seems to me, Ryder, that there is the making of a very pretty villain in you.'

relationships.description: Ryder confesses his crimes and pleads for mercy to Holmes.
relationships.context: 'I will tell you it just as it happened, sir,' said he. 'When Horner had been arrested, it seemed to me that it would be best for me to get away with the stone at once, for I did not know at what moment the police might not take it into their heads to search me and my room.'

relationships.description: Ryder frames Horner for the jewel theft to divert suspicion.
relationships.context: 'You knew that this man Horner, the plumber, had been concerned in some such matter before, and that suspicion would rest the more readily upon him.'

relationships.description: Horner lives in fear of Ryder's accusations and the resulting arrest.
relationships.context: 'It is very well to cringe and crawl now, but you thought little enough of this poor Horner in the dock for a crime of which he knew nothing.'

relationships.description: Ryder hires or utilizes Cusack as an accomplice for the heist.
relationships.context: 'You made some small job in my lady's room-you and your confederate Cusack-and you managed that he should be the man sent for.'

relationships.description: Cusack acts as an accomplice to Ryder during the jewel robbery.
relationships.context: 'You made some small job in my lady's room-you and your confederate Cusack-and you managed that he should be the man sent for.'

relationships.description: Maggie is married to Oakshott and lives with him.
relationships.context: 'She had married a man named Oakshott, and lived in Brixton Road, where she fattened fowls for the market.'

relationships.description: Oakshott is the husband of Maggie Oakshott.
relationships.context: 'She had married a man named Oakshott, and lived in Brixton Road, where she fattened fowls for the market.'

relationships.description: Ryder knows Maggie as his sister and seeks her help to hide the stolen stone.
relationships.context: 'I went out, as if on some commission, and I made for my sister's house. She had married a man named Oakshott, and lived in Brixton Road, where she fattened fowls for the market.'

relationships.description: Maggie knows Ryder as her brother and provides him with geese.
relationships.context: 'My sister asked me what was the matter, and why I was so pale; but I told her that I had been upset by the jewel robbery at the hotel.'

relationships.description: Ryder knows Maudsley as a former criminal associate who can help fence the stone.
relationships.context: 'I had a friend once called Maudsley, who went to the bad, and has just been serving his time in Pentonville. One day he had met me, and fell into talk about the ways of thieves, and how they could get rid of what they stole.'

relationships.description: Maudsley knows Ryder as a friend and fellow thief.
relationships.context: 'I knew that he would be true to me, for I knew one or two things about him; so I made up my mind to go right on to Kilburn, where he lived, and take him into my confidence.'

relationships.description: Watson assists Holmes as his companion and chronicler during the investigation.
relationships.context: 'After all, Watson,' said Holmes, reaching up his hand for his clay pipe, 'I am not retained by the police to supply their deficiencies.'

relationships.description: Holmes relies on Watson's presence and support during the interrogation.
relationships.context: 'After all, Watson,' said Holmes, reaching up his hand for his clay pipe, 'I am not retained by the police to supply their deficiencies.'

relationships.description: Ryder fears the police will search him and discover the stolen stone.
relationships.context: 'When Horner had been arrested, it seemed to me that it would be best for me to get away with the stone at once, for I did not know at what moment the police might not take it into their heads to search me and my room.'

relationships.description: The Police arrest and imprison Horner based on false suspicion.
relationships.context: 'Then, when he had left, you rifled the jewel-case, raised the alarm, and had this unfortunate man arrested.'

relationships.description: The Police investigate Ryder as the prime suspect in the jewel robbery.
relationships.context: 'I did not know at what moment the police might not take it into their heads to search me and my room.'

relationships.description: Maggie Oakshott lives at Brixton Road with her husband.
relationships.context: 'She had married a man named Oakshott, and lived in Brixton Road, where she fattened fowls for the market.'

relationships.description: Ryder visits Brixton Road to hide the stolen stone with his sister.
relationships.context: 'I went out, as if on some commission, and I made for my sister's house. She had married a man named Oakshott, and lived in Brixton Road, where she fattened fowls for the market.'

relationships.description: Maudsley was recently imprisoned at Pentonville prison.
relationships.context: 'I had a friend once called Maudsley, who went to the bad, and has just been serving his time in Pentonville.'

relationships.description: Maudsley resides in Kilburn, where Ryder seeks him out.
relationships.context: 'so I made up my mind to go right on to Kilburn, where he lived, and take him into my confidence.'

relationships.description: Breckinridge operates as a dealer in Covent Garden.
relationships.context: 'Gone to the dealer's, Jem.' 'Which dealer's?' 'Breckinridge, of Covent Garden.'

relationships.description: The Market operates out of Covent Garden, where the geese are sold.
relationships.context: 'Breckinridge, of Covent Garden.' 'Well, he has always answered me like that.'

relationships.description: Ryder hides The Stone inside a goose to evade police detection.
relationships.context: 'I caught it, and prying its bill open, I thrust the stone down its throat as far as my finger could reach.'

relationships.description: Ryder uses The Goose as a container to transport the stolen stone.
relationships.context: 'I would take my goose now, and in it I would carry my stone to Kilburn.'

relationships.description: Ryder uses The Bible as a prop to swear an oath of innocence and plea for mercy.
relationships.context: 'I swear it. I'll swear it on a Bible. Oh, don't bring it into court!'"

relationships.description: Holmes uses his clay pipe while reflecting on the case's resolution.
relationships.context: 'After all, Watson,' said Holmes, reaching up his hand for his clay pipe, 'I am not retained by the police to supply their deficiencies.'

relationships.description: The Stone is the central object stolen during the jewel robbery.
relationships.context: 'Then, when he had left, you rifled the jewel-case, raised the alarm, and had this unfortunate man arrested.'

relationships.description: The Goose is used to smuggle the stolen stone, becoming a key element of the robbery's aftermath.
relationships.context: 'I would take my goose now, and in it I would carry my stone to Kilburn.'

relationships.description: The Bible is used as a sacred object during Ryder's confession and plea for mercy.
relationships.context: 'I swear it. I'll swear it on a Bible. Oh, don't bring it into court!'"

relationships.description: Ryder is the primary perpetrator of the jewel robbery.
relationships.context: 'You made some small job in my lady's room-you and your confederate Cusack-and you managed that he should be the man sent for.'

relationships.description: Horner is an unwitting participant and victim of the jewel robbery.
relationships.context: 'It is very well to cringe and crawl now, but you thought little enough of this poor Horner in the dock for a crime of which he knew nothing.'

relationships.description: The Police investigate the jewel robbery and arrest a suspect.
relationships.context: 'I am not retained by the police to supply their deficiencies. If Horner were in danger it would be another thing; but this fellow will not appear against him, and the case must collapse.'

relationships.description: Ryder orchestrates the arrest of Horner to cover his own tracks.
relationships.context: 'Then, when he had left, you rifled the jewel-case, raised the alarm, and had this unfortunate man arrested.'

relationships.description: Horner is the victim arrested for the jewel robbery.
relationships.context: 'Then, when he had left, you rifled the jewel-case, raised the alarm, and had this unfortunate man arrested.'

relationships.description: Ryder confesses his crimes to Holmes and pleads for leniency.
relationships.context: 'I will tell you it just as it happened, sir,' said he. 'When Horner had been arrested, it seemed to me that it would be best for me to get away with the stone at once, for I did not know at what moment the police might not take it into their heads to search me and my room.'

relationships.description: Holmes interrogates Ryder and listens to his confession.
relationships.context: 'Get back into your chair!' said Holmes sternly. 'It is very well to cringe and crawl now, but you thought little enough of this poor Horner in the dock for a crime of which he knew nothing.'

relationships.description: Ryder is released by Holmes and flees the scene.
relationships.context: 'Get out!' said he. 'What, sir! Oh, Heaven bless you!' 'No more words. Get out!'"

relationships.description: Holmes orders Ryder's release, choosing mercy over imprisonment.
relationships.context: 'After all, Watson,' said Holmes, reaching up his hand for his clay pipe, 'I am not retained by the police to supply their deficiencies. If Horner were in danger it would be another thing; but this fellow will not appear against him, and the case must collapse.'

relationships.description: The temptation of sudden wealth motivates Ryder to commit the jewel robbery.
relationships.context: 'Well, the temptation of sudden wealth so easily acquired was too much for you, as it has been for better men before you; but you were not very scrupulous in the means you used.'

relationships.description: Horner's past reputation makes him a target for suspicion during the investigation.
relationships.context: 'You knew that this man Horner, the plumber, had been concerned in some such matter before, and that suspicion would rest the more readily upon him.'

relationships.description: Holmes expresses mercy and salvation by choosing to release Ryder rather than send him to prison.
relationships.context: 'I suppose that I am commuting a felony, but it is just possible that I am saving a soul. This fellow will not go wrong again; he is too terribly frightened.'

relationships.description: Ryder is involved in crime and felony, which Holmes considers commuting.
relationships.context: 'I suppose that I am commuting a felony, but it is just possible that I am saving a soul.'

relationships.description: The prospect of sudden wealth triggers Ryder's criminal actions and moral downfall.
relationships.context: 'Well, the temptation of sudden wealth so easily acquired was too much for you, as it has been for better men before you; but you were not very scrupulous in the means you used.'

relationships.description: Mercy and salvation resolve Ryder's predicament, sparing him from a life of crime and prison.
relationships.context: 'For God's sake, have mercy!' he shrieked. 'Think of my father! Of my mother! It would break their hearts.'

relationships.description: The Police arrest Horner based on circumstantial evidence and suspicion.
relationships.context: 'Then, when he had left, you rifled the jewel-case, raised the alarm, and had this unfortunate man arrested.'

relationships.description: Ryder visits My Lady's Room to commit the jewel theft.
relationships.context: 'You made some small job in my lady's room-you and your confederate Cusack-and you managed that he should be the man sent for.'

relationships.description: The Stone is stolen from My Lady's Room during the robbery.
relationships.context: 'Then, when he had left, you rifled the jewel-case, raised the alarm, and had this unfortunate man arrested.'

relationships.description: Cusack assists Ryder in committing the jewel robbery.
relationships.context: 'You made some small job in my lady's room-you and your confederate Cusack-and you managed that he should be the man sent for.'

relationships.description: Maudsley is involved in the aftermath of the jewel robbery by helping fence the stone.
relationships.context: 'I had a friend once called Maudsley, who went to the bad, and has just been serving his time in Pentonville. One day he had met me, and fell into talk about the ways of thieves, and how they could get rid of what they stole.'

relationships.description: Ryder flees from Brixton Road after being released by Holmes.
relationships.context: 'And no more words were needed. There was a rush, a clatter upon the stairs, the bang of a door, and the crisp rattle of running footfalls from the street.'

relationships.description: The Market is represented by Breckinridge, who acts as its dealer.
relationships.context: 'Gone to the dealer's, Jem.' 'Which dealer's?' 'Breckinridge, of Covent Garden.'
